item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Winning a cliché contest with a cliché that you didn't intend to use is like winning an ugly sweater contest at a party without realizing there was an ugly sweater contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know what they say about modern problems

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this, the characters are all loveable and I didn't see the "it's an Amazon echo" twist coming.

It also qualifies for the story cliché "entity is cryptic, followed by the interviewer suffering" lmao, I can't believe this was not on purpose because it's a perfect tongue-in-cheek parody of that cliché.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I must admit, there is something strangely appealing about a foul-mouthed, foul-tempered Scottish gnome. It just seems right, somehow. Like others have said, I would have liked to see more of his 'character'.

One little niggle - you might want to give the proofreading another shot. There are quite a few instances (primarily in the various addendums/logs) of minor spelling mistakes, words either missing or added, wrong tense, etc. Nothing major, it's just that those small mistakes, when added together, disturb the narrative too much for me to believe it any more.

Staying neutral for now - my likes and dislikes cancel each other out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the entry altogether, but the journal does seem to be out-of-place, as mentioned above.
Given that skips are meant to be more mysterious, it gives almost a bit too much information than is necessary to understand the object.

If you intend to write more skips involved with this character, it would serve more of a purpose when used to establish the lore they share. Recovered journal fragments shared among multiple skips could be an interesting tool to establish the character of your Antiquer across those articles.

Aaanyway, +1 cause u gnomed me :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You might get a bit more of this. I plan on starting a series of connected skips/tales based off of the Antiquer, I might feature the gnome some more in a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Think you accidentally a word at the end there: "since the last time I tried working with intelligence, I should probably that in the future."

Also, is "apartment complex" the sort of language a Scot would typically use? "block of flats" or just "flats" (or "tower block", or either word in isolation, for a tall one) is more Commonwealthy, and for a certain urban subset of the Scottish population it'd be "tenement"…

Generally though, like Emil, I find myself wanting a bit more of this (like, maybe the Foundation get more friendly with the gnome, start working together, use his electrokinesis powers in anger, build him a little robot body…), which is probably a good sign.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, the guy did just copy the engrams of a half drunk, half hungover scot who was by his own admission already in a bad mood…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a really interesting read and I’ve got to say I was hooked, the idea’s inventive and unique, I really hope you have a chance for winning the competition!
Probably the best part was the Gnome’s outbursts and general standoffishness, it paints quie a character.
My only ‘problem’ with the article would be that the Journal transcript might have been unnecessary, I’m not too sure why but it might be that it’s relaying information we were told before or it could have just been that too much was explained. But in some ways the different sides of the story makes it more interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The gnome's dialogue is fantastic, I just wish there was more documentation of his outbursts. Maybe a researcher says the wrong thing and makes him flip his lid?

I also feel this article would work way better with a picture. It shouldn't be too difficult to find a picture of a garden gnome, and it would probably make his outbursts way funnier, since you'd know how mismatched they would be with his appearance.

Upvoted, because it's impressive how you wrote a humorous article without making it feel like a -J.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not gonna lie, I was fully expecting to get gnomed at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, sorry about that. I don't know how I glossed over it, the formatting is fixed now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
4650 shows minor signs of age

You should always refer to anomalies by their SCP-XXXX designation, nicknames and numbers are colloquial.

I'm also not too sure that the journal entry added anything new, and I felt like it made it drag a bit.

That said, it was interesting, so +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Happy Cliché-Con! I had a general idea for a story like this for a few weeks, but couldn't think of a specific way to implement it. About a week ago I got an idea for it, and wrote the bulk of the article in a single day. After several days of proofreading, going through drafts, and critiquing, here it is! This is a Category 1 submission.

Show Cliché (Spoilers)
Hide Cliché

An item that is very good at doing the thing its supposed to do, too good in fact.

Thanks for reading!
